speaker: interviewee
text: Where you going to be? Sit here. So you an listen-- but you have to do it right now because you can't go through later. You'll be making noise.

speaker: interviewer
text: I go around this way.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you can't when they-- you make noise.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] be comfortable. We can sit-- move that stuff. And you can sit in that chair.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it--

speaker: interviewer
text: February 6, 1995, interview with Regina Gelb by Chana Meier in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the interview's in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] start over? Mrs. Gelb?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell us your full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Regina Gelb, born Laks. I also have a nickname. And I go by the name of [? Rainya ?] as well as Regina.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That is a nickname that derives, really, out of Regina.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what town were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in central Poland, in Starachowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. S-T-A-R-A-C-H-O-W-I-C-E. It's very difficult to say-- pronounce even worse. However, it is known-- it was known, in Poland since the First World War, as a matter of fact, since 1918, because that town became a-- one of the industrial centers of the newborn independent Poland after the First War. And the claim to fame, so to say, for the town was that it had a factory that was basically smelting ore, and producing locomotives and meat grinders and such. And the importance of it all is the fact that when the Second War broke out and the Germans occupied Poland, that factory was immediately converted to war production. And this became a munitions factory. And by virtue of that, there was a slave labor camp established in my hometown. And Jews who were either left in the town after the town was cleared of the Jewish population, or anybody else from surrounding towns who was able-bodied, was shipped into Starachowice, into slave labor camps in which we lived, where there were three shifts operating the factory. And this was canons for artillery is-- was the basic production during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, tell me a little about your family life before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. I come from a family of-- of course father, mother, and three sisters. I am the youngest of the three. My mother's name was Poline or Pola. Her maiden name Tenenblum. She came from central Poland, [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE]. My father was-- his name was Isaac Laks. And he came from [INAUDIBLE], which is Western Poland. And the difference in their backgrounds was that my father was really educated in the German language. Even though he was a Polish citizen, his basic and higher education was in German. So my father spoke German fluently. And he had his encyclopedia in German and his-- his literature was German. But his Polish, of course, was beautiful. Mother's on the other hand, was-- had a mixture of the Russian, because as everyone knows from history, Poland was, prior to the First War, divided into three units, Russian, German, and Austrian. My mother came from the so-called Russian part. Anyhow, my father, as I said, came from [INAUDIBLE] in the western part, mother from central Poland from a very large family. And mother's-- my grandmother, Tenenblum, had 13 children. She bore 13 children, nine of whom survived. And these, of course, like my mother, had their own families. So that gives you an idea how large my mother's family was. And of course, all of them perished. And well, we lived a very good life. We had a very, very kind of a comfortable life. My parents-- my mother was very much involved in the Zionist movement. She was president of the Women's International Zionist Organization, WIZO, for the district, whether it was the-- I don't know how large the district was, but it probably encompassed number of towns. My father was an engineer in the forestry lumber industry. And both were very well educated. And the three of us, my oldest sister is Anna, presently Wilson. She also had a nickname, [? Hanya. ?] The second sister, the middle one, Rosalie [? Kristine ?] Lerman, also known as [? Krisha. ?] And I am the third one. And we all attended school. We attended Polish schools, not Jewish schools, because our town also had a very sizable Jewish population. And the religious part of the Jewish population preferred their own schools. And they had religious schools for girls. And they had religious schools for boys. We did not attend any of those schools. We went to the regular Polish school. And the language spoken at home was Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was just a public school. You know, this was just a regular public school in town. It had no name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- when the war broke out, I was 10 years old. I never, of course, never finished elementary school before the war. My oldest sister, Anna, she was already in gymnasium, which is high school, which was a higher level in Poland than it is here. And my second sister of-- the middle sister, of course, had more education than I. But my education was cut off right in the middle of-- of elementary school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like go to school with other Polish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I adored it, because we were very much in tune with our surroundings. We did not live a-- a-- a ghetto-ized type of existence. We mingled with the Christians. We attended their Christmas dinners and-- and went to look at their Christmas trees. And they would come for Passovers to our home. And we just felt ourselves part of the community, even though we were Jewish and participated in the Jewish community. But not-- my parents were only religious to an extent that, you know, they attended synagogue and such. But they were not-- we were not kosher, you see. They were not religious. However, they had a very strong Jewish identity. And this is how my mother was so involved with the Zionist movement, because that was their way of-- of reaffirming what they considered themselves to be. And in fact, I went after my regular school-- excuse me-- I went to a Hebrew afternoon school, which was a secular school, you see, to learn the language and to study the poems of [INAUDIBLE] and such. And it was very interesting because it was just one more language to learn. And-- and of course, that ended with the war, as my education, my formal Polish education, ended because the Jewish children were not allowed to participate in schools. Excuse me. Of course, there's always a way to manage, otherwise. I have a frog in my throat. Excuse me. Anyhow, what happened is because my oldest sister had all of the books at home, and we children were really not allowed in the streets for the reason that the Germans who-- the gestapo were always around with dogs. And it was just absolute, total terror for a child to be outside and see these-- these marching-- you know, the German-- the gestapo with the dogs, they sort of had a-- they-- they had a certain posture. And just looking at it was frightening.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what we did is-- I'll take a little water. [DRINKS] We stayed home and my oldest sister, Anna, organized little classes, you see. And of course, whatever books were in the house, and there were plenty of those, including manuals, because she had all her school books. So we learned-- I learned Latin. And I learned French. And I learned chemistry. And I wasn't even out of elementary school yet. But of course, we're talking about-- this is from 1939 after the war broke out, up to the time when we were-- when the town was liquidated in October of 1942, which is a stretch of-- of two years. And certainly, this was no way of-- this wasn't us killing time. You see? This was a protecting the children from being outside. We couldn't go swimming, because children weren't-- we were not allowed to go to the river. There was a river, [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] is a tributary to the Vistula.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we used to go swimming in the [INAUDIBLE], but not during the war. We couldn't travel on trains, that was forbidden. So that was out. Going to Polish school was out. So what remained wasn't killing time, was really using the resources that were available. And frankly, I think I had my-- the-- the underlying foundation for my later higher education is really based on what I have acquired during those days, those two years locked up in a house. And of course, it kept us very stimulated, intellectually. And probably away from the fear and the horror, and all the other things that were going out-- on outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, before the outset of the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you experience any of that fear, any of that anti-Semitism in the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it is sort of a difficult question because, you understand, I was very young. I was not in any way persecuted. We were well accepted in town, even though toward the end of the '30s there were boycotts of Jewish stores. We did not own stores. I mean, my father was not a storekeeper.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that-- I did know, as a child, that there were boycotts-- boycotts of Jewish stores. And then there was anti-Semitism. But in my personal contact with my world, I really wasn't too much aware of it, you see. But I ascribe it really to my age, rather than to my understanding of the situation. [DRINKS] Do you remember other members of your extended family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you used to get together on holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Well, not really. You see, my grandmother, who I refer to my mother's mother, her name was Sarah Tenenblum, lived about 20 miles away from us in [INAUDIBLE]. And she and-- had two daughters that weren't yet married, my Aunt Regina, after whom I am-- I have a name, and my-- and Anjia owned a printing shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [INAUDIBLE], as we called her, grandma, [INAUDIBLE], used to invite each one of us, of the three sisters, for a stay of a week or two with her alone. You know? She was religious, and she felt she would supplement the religious education that perhaps my mother did not supply to the extent that she like-- would have liked it. And well, my times spent with the grandmother were just absolutely out of this world, for me, at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, we're talking about my being eight or nine. I would go, of course be taken into the printing shop. I was allowed to go to the-- where the facility, the operation of the-- of the-- of the painting was taking place. I was allowed to-- to touch the machinery because the gentleman who worked in the back knew me, and of course they-- they wanted to impress me. And they did. And then on the way home-- before I would go home, I would collect a bag of-- they would collect it for me, you know, when they would print, let's say, posters. And they would cut them. And all of the thing-- the paper strips that would fall off would be put in a bag. And then if they did ledgers, and they puncture these round holes, which made a bag of confetti. Well of course, I was-- I was absolutely it with a capital I when I came back home. And I had these bags of this wonderful stuff. And of course, I think I was injected with printer's ink because I'm a very-- I'm an avid reader, and I have always adored reading. And I think this is where it really came from. I-- I really-- this is it. Absolutely. [DRINKS] Grandmother's-- my grandmother herself, though she was very religious, was very well educated for a woman of her time. She spoke French fluently, and she came from a very fine family. And as-- she bore 13 children, nine of whom survived. And unfortunately, after the war was over, we found out that nobody from that family in Poland survived. And I really adored those two aunts, my Aunt Regina, very much. And we used to visit, also, as a family-- the family on my father's side. Except they lived in Sosnowiec, you see. So-- but my father's brother lived in Radom. And that was closer. And so we would visit with that family. And they would come to us. But going to Sosnowiec was a little difficult and the other grandmother-- by the way, I never knew my grandfathers, not one and not the other. But the other grandmother would come to stay with us for a-- for an extended visit. And whatever, a few weeks. But we were very close knit family, all around, on both sides.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was a holiday like in your house? Did you attend synagogue with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not really. Not really. My parents attended a synagogue, but it was sort of a progressive type of-- this was a small town, you see. It was a progress of type of synagogue. And of course, they fasted on Yom Kippur and all this. But basically, our religious life was not the same as the other people in town who were basically Hasidim, you know. And they stood out in the-- from the population. They lived their own life. And they-- they didn't speak Polish, you see. So-- but there was, of course, a whole group of people who were my parents' kind of-- people with my parents' attitude. So it wasn't that we stood out and we were just alone. There were an awful lot of people who-- who were about on the same level, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said your mother was very active in the Zionist movement. When things started getting progressively worse, did your family make any plans to move to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. In those days, people who went to Palestine were what you called pioneers. Basically, these were single people, unattached, whether it was men or women. And a family like ours, you know, we had a very, very fine life. And there was nowhere to go, really. And Palestine, in those days, wasn't what you would call a place to bring up children. The land was arid. People lived in kibbutzes, and they were-- kibbutzim, and they were first, you know, acquiring land. So no that-- no, that-- the answer is no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think your parents were aware of how bad it was gonna be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, frankly I don't know. I am sure they were aware because they were educated. They read. And my father, as I mentioned before, read a lot of German publications. So he most certainly was aware. But as far as I'm concerned, I really did not know-- and as a matter of fact, to this day, I am still wondering how much my parents knew during the war, to the very last minute. I think they have protected us so from knowing the truth of what was going on. That to this-- this day I am forever uncertain whether they really knew that Treblinka existed, that Auschwitz existed. That all of these deportations meant really going to the gas chamber. I really couldn't answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did a lot of people in your community try to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Absolutely not. As a matter of fact, our community was picked for a resettlement center, you know, like people from Lodz. You see, we lived in a ghetto. Yes. But this is much later. Prior to the Second War, as far as I remember, I only remember a neighbor or ours who went to Argentina and everybody was saying very quietly, he went bankrupt, had to run away to Argentina. I don't know what his name is, but this is how I knew about Argentina before the war because of this man ran away after being bankrupt. No, nobody really run away. Life was lived, you know, as it is lived, with all the good and the bad and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the was break-- broke out in 1939, what happened to your community? What happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, nothing happened. The Germans walked in. The town was evacuated, because they-- everybody thought the factory's gonna be blown to smithereens because based on the-- on information on experience from the Second War, the enemy who occupies, he was gonna bomb you to threads, right? Well, this never happened. My hometown was never bombed. We were evacuated into the surrounding forest, thinking that this really is going to happen. Little did we know that the factory became operational within one week. The Germans must have had spies there, or however they knew of its existence, because immediately, as soon as-- as so-called the-- the-- the sirens stopped wailing, we got back home. Nothing was touched. And the factory was taken over and immediately changed to war production.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were evacuated to the forest, did you take any belongings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. We just took clothes and food. And we-- we marched. It was-- this is a-- a-- a-- it's very, very strong impression left on me. And I remember it to this day because the town was evacuated together with the surrounding villages. So we were caught in the cows and people and-- and-- and horses and peasants and the Jews, and everybody mixed in on the road into the forest. And during the night-- of course, we thought, meanwhile, that Starachowice was just gonna be wiped off the earth. And meanwhile we were sitting in these forests. And the German airplanes came down with the reflectors on their airplanes. And they were shooting people down in the forest, because, you know, there's a clearing. And people would go to sleep. And the cows would-- you would hear the cows. And you would hear the dogs. And whatever it is, whether they heard it in the airplanes or not, they came and they shot us. And finally-- of course, we were not hit during that night shooting when they would sort of pick us-- pick up the groups that they wanted to shoot at. But eventually, as we moved on, we were caught in a valley between the Polish units on one side, on a sort of-- it wasn't a hill-- in a type of hilly area-- and the German units on the other. We were caught in the middle, in the crossfire. And this was just a horrendously frightening experience, because this was really my first war experience. And mother insisted that we will wear our best clothes, the newest. I had a pair of shoes, brand new shows bought for the Jewish holidays. And I put on these shoes. That was the biggest mistake I ever made, because very soon-- it was a very hot, this September, very extremely hot. We were overdressed because we wore all that stuff. Mother said, you know, it might be cold, whatever. I had the worst blisters and had to take my shoes off. And the PS to this whole story is that after being shot at by the German airplanes that we lowering-- lowered themselves over forest, after being caught-- caught in the crossfire, we came back and found out that nothing happened in town, absolutely nothing. One bomb fell somewhere that didn't even destroy anything. And that was sort of like a-- I don't know if it was a reawakening, but maybe this was a sign that things are not what they seem to be, or whatever it is that you are expecting might not really turn out that way, because in fact, that September 1939 is when whatever was planned-- anti-aircraft were digging ditches-- whatever was done, none of that was used. People were killed by whatever happened that was not expected. And we came back. And-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We went back to our home. And everything was left. In fact, we ate and left everything, because you know, the evacuation was announced. And-- and we came back, everything was there. Everything was there. The only thing is, of course, that we lived on the main street. And this is 1939, September 1939. And we were allowed to live on that street for a few more months. There was no special clothes in ghetto organized in Starachowice, but what they did is they cleared that street, which was called [INAUDIBLE], named after the Polish Marshal Pilsudski. That street was cleared, and we were among the first ones to have to leave our home, and got resettled closer to the-- like there was a square. And then eventually, we found ourselves again in the wrong place, because they were clearing that street. And we had three more residences, three, after we left our original residence. We had three more residences in the same town, but not in an enclosed ghetto. The Poles who lived where they lived were allowed to stay. However, the Jews were being squeezed out of certain streets into that other district. As a matter of fact, the last place where we lived was-- it was a Christian family. He was a teamster. Had a horse and wagon. And they had a little house. And they gave us one big room with a kitchen. And they had the other part of the house. And I will never forget-- the wife was pregnant. And she was working the horses. And she was washing the horses and feeding them and all. And-- and then I see my mother-- I know that the woman was gonna have a baby. I mean, I-- I knew just that much. But all of a sudden, mother shushed me and she showed-- she told us to go somewhere. And she was cooking boiling water. The woman was with the horses five minutes before, and she was giving birth five minutes after. And my mother was attending her. And of course, the children shouldn't know what it was or how it was. You know, because-- Anyhow, so these were people of another-- they were-- they were peasant type, but very decent people. And there was a little boy, I remember. I played with him in the snow. But mother helped her give birth to the child. And believe me, she must have just walked out of the barn where she cleaned the horses and fed them, and went and had the baby. That's-- that's exactly how it was. Anyhow, it was from that last apartment with the-- where we lived with the teamsters that the final solution came in our hometown. So that was our last residence.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the beginning of ghetto life, was your father allowed to continue working?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, absolutely not. My father then was given some kind of a job in a-- as a scribe, you know, because he-- he was very literate. He was a scribe somewhere. But this-- those were very difficult times, very.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even when you came back to your original home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, yes. Because right way, as I mentioned before, we were cut off from everything. I mean, the children were cut off from school. But the men were cut off from their livelihood, especially a man of my father's position. Of course, storekeepers still had stores and such, but our existence changed completely, totally. You see. And of course, in the ghetto, there were stores where-- there were-- food-- food was rationed. And there was constant fear. There was constant fear. We didn't know what was going to happen. And still there-- there was, with all this fear and with all the uncertainty, and with all the anticipation of something horrible to come, there was the life, you know. There was, as I told you, the classes that were conducted. I do remember there was a very transport of people from Lodz sent to our hometown. And soon enough, my sisters found friends among them. And they were budding poets or whatever. I still remember hearing Lord Byron's poetry read in Polish. I mean, really. And they had these-- these evenings, these literary evenings, and there was a semblance of normal life, though-- even though it really wasn't normal, the make believe made it so, because still there was this exchange of-- of ideas, of-- of culture, you see. And as long as we were still at home, meaning whichever home it was, whether it was the first or the last, there was this semblance. For example, I remember my mother made a-- my-- Regina was engaged and Mother made the party for her. And she put little pieces so fern on the table cloth. And she made it pretty. You know, those things counted. Those were the things that really uplifted you. And of course, unfortunately, from that last apartment is where we-- everything changed after that.
